case_name: Mevinphos removal from river-derived surface water for drinking water with minimal chemical addition
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for Mevinphos-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water production under a minimal-chemical-use preference.

input_data.scenario_background: A small drinking water plant abstracts from a river reach downstream of agricultural land where an organophosphate pesticide pulse has been detected after seasonal runoff. Mevinphos is the main acute concern, while the source water also contains moderate turbidity and natural organic matter typical of surface water. The utility prefers treatment approaches that minimize added chemicals, remain practical for conventional plant staff, and provide reliable protection for potable supply.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve strong reduction of Mevinphos and comply with drinking water safety expectations for pesticide control while maintaining microbiological protection.
secondary_goals: minimize routine chemical addition where feasible
use established drinking water treatment technology

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: backwash water and spent adsorbent handling are manageable, but large chemical sludge generation is undesirable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: treatment should remain dependable during variable runoff events and moderate source-water quality swings

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove large debris and protect downstream units
core_unit_functions.media_filtration_or_low_chemical_clarifi: reduce turbidity and particulate natural organic matter that can interfere with adsorption
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb dissolved Mevinphos and provide polishing of taste, odor, and trace organics
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: Mevinphos is an organic pesticide for which activated carbon is a well-established drinking water control approach
surface water matrix effects from DOC and turbidity make pretreatment beneficial before adsorption
this train aligns with the stated preference for minimal chemical use because the main contaminant barrier is adsorption rather than intensive chemical oxidation
GAC is widely used for trace organic micropollutants and can be integrated into conventional municipal treatment practice
potential_risks: high DOC or pesticide spikes can shorten GAC bed life and require closer monitoring
if pretreatment is inadequate, solids loading may foul GAC and reduce adsorption performance
spent carbon replacement or reactivation planning is required

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove gross solids and stabilize downstream operation
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_dosing: provide flexible adsorption of dissolved Mevinphos during contamination episodes
core_unit_functions.filtration: polish residual suspended matter and carbon fines

applicability_rationale: PAC is a proven emergency and seasonal-response option for pesticide events in surface water treatment
it offers operational flexibility when contamination occurs as a runoff-driven pulse rather than continuously
the process is well known in drinking water practice and can provide rapid deployment compared with full-scale biological or membrane alternatives
potential_risks: this option uses more chemicals than GAC and therefore fits the minimal-chemical-use preference less well
PAC dose optimization can be sensitive to DOC competition and contact time
carbon-carrying sludge production increases solids handling needs

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove large debris and protect equipment
core_unit_functions.roughing_or_media_filtration: lower turbidity to support downstream oxidation and BAC performance
core_unit_functions.ozonation_or_uv_based_advanced_oxidation: transform Mevinphos and other oxidizable organics
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb remaining trace organics and biologically polish biodegradable oxidation products

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can be technically effective for organophosphate pesticides when adsorption alone is insufficient
BAC provides an established follow-on polishing barrier after oxidation for trace organics in surface water
this route may be attractive when multiple taste, odor, or micropollutant issues coexist
potential_risks: chemical and energy demand are higher than adsorption-led options
oxidation by-products and partial transformation products require evaluation
advanced oxidation control is less aligned with the stated low-to-medium operating complexity

core_unit_functions.bank_filtration_or_managed_natural_pretr: attenuate particulates and some organic matter through natural subsurface passage
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: remove residual dissolved Mevinphos and other trace organics

applicability_rationale: this approach strongly supports the minimal-chemical-use objective where site conditions allow natural pretreatment
natural attenuation plus GAC can provide a robust multi-barrier concept for surface water impacted by agricultural runoff
it is a recognized water supply strategy in some riverbank abstraction settings
potential_risks: site hydrogeology may not support reliable bank filtration performance
response time to short pesticide pulses may be variable depending on travel time and subsurface conditions
not all utilities have the intake configuration or land access needed

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_filtration: reduce particulate loading and protect membranes
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide broad-spectrum rejection of dissolved contaminants including pesticides
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: high-pressure membranes are technically capable of controlling many dissolved organics including pesticide contaminants
they may be selected when a utility also needs desalting or broad removal of multiple dissolved contaminants
potential_risks: membrane concentrate management adds a significant disposal burden
capital and energy demand are disproportionate for a case centered on one primary pesticide contaminant
this option does not fit the minimal-chemical-use and moderate-complexity preference as well as adsorption-based treatment

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based treatment should rank first for Mevinphos under minimal chemical use constraints
flexible PAC treatment may rank high but below GAC because it involves routine chemical addition
high-pressure membrane options should not rank first for this straightforward pesticide-removal case

must_reflect: preference for minimal routine chemical addition
surface water pretreatment needs due to turbidity and DOC matrix effects
use of established municipal drinking water technology rather than unnecessarily intensive processes

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for organic pesticide removal
why DOC and turbidity can reduce adsorption efficiency and justify pretreatment
why oxidation-based or membrane-heavy options rank lower under the stated constraints

should_mention: competition from natural organic matter for adsorption sites
shortened carbon life during contamination spikes
possible transformation by-products if oxidation is used
residuals or spent carbon management needs
